_meta.description: Canonical material alias map for the Oil Painting Research Assistant. Maps alias terms to canonical names for materials mentioned in sources and chunks. Used during ingestion, metadata extraction, and query normalization. The canonical_name field is the value stored in materials_mentioned fields.

ambiguity_warnings: Flake white sometimes refers to a specific product formulation, not pure PW1
Cremnitz white is a distinct formulation of lead white with different particle morphology
materials.notes: Historical pigment. High density. Historically dominant white in oil painting. Associated with conservation concerns in modern use.

ambiguity_warnings: Chinese white typically refers to the watercolor formulation, not oil
Do not conflate zinc_white with titanium_white — different chemistry and handling
materials.notes: Associated with brittleness concerns in oil paint films. Risk varies by formulation, proportion, and application context.

ambiguity_warnings: Do not conflate titanium_white with zinc_white or lead_white — distinct material properties
materials.notes: Modern dominant white. High opacity, high tinting strength. First commercially available early 20th century — anachronistic in pre-20th century historical context.

ambiguity_warnings: Distinguish from natural_ultramarine (lapis lazuli, PB29 natural) — different cost, availability, and historical use
Queries about 'ultramarine' in historical context may refer to natural_ultramarine
materials.notes: Synthetic version developed 1828. Broadly lightfast. Modern standard blue.

ambiguity_warnings: PB29 is used for both natural and synthetic ultramarine — distinguish in historical context
Queries about ultramarine before 1828 refer to this material
materials.notes: Historically the most expensive pigment. Derived from lapis lazuli. Widely used in medieval and Renaissance painting.

ambiguity_warnings: Distinct from mars_yellow (synthetic iron oxide) even when both appear as yellow earth tones
materials.notes: Natural earth pigment. Historically universal. Lightfast and stable.

ambiguity_warnings: Distinguish from burnt_umber — different color and handling properties
materials.notes: Fast dryer due to manganese content. Natural earth pigment.

ambiguity_warnings: Distinguish from raw_umber — darker, warmer, still a fast dryer but different color
materials.notes: Produced by calcining raw umber. Fast dryer. Widely used for underpainting and glazing.

ambiguity_warnings: Distinguish from burnt_sienna — different color and transparency
materials.notes: Transparent yellow-brown earth. Historical pigment.

ambiguity_warnings: Distinguish from raw_sienna — warmer, more transparent red-brown
materials.notes: Produced by calcining raw sienna. Warm transparent red-brown.

ambiguity_warnings: Modern paints labelled terre verte may contain PG7 or PG36 rather than PG23 — check TDS
materials.notes: Historically used in flesh shadow underpaintings, particularly in Italian and Flemish practice. Weak tinting strength.

ambiguity_warnings: Distinct from chrome_oxide_green (PG17) — viridian is transparent, chrome oxide is opaque
materials.notes: Transparent, cool, lightfast green. Introduced mid-19th century.

ambiguity_warnings: Historical pigment — not equivalent to any modern synthetic pigment
Prone to discoloration in oil — document source when citing claims about stability
materials.notes: Historical copper acetate pigment. Used in medieval and Renaissance painting. Known to discolor and degrade in oil media.

ambiguity_warnings: Not the same as cobalt_blue (PB28) — completely different material
Smalt discoloration is a well-documented conservation issue
materials.notes: Historical blue glass pigment. Cobalt-containing. Known to discolor (decolorize) over time. Common in 15th–18th century European painting.

ambiguity_warnings: Modern ivory_black is typically bone black (calcined bone), not true ivory — manufacturers vary
materials.notes: Slow dryer. Warm black with brownish undertone. Historical and modern use.

ambiguity_warnings: Distinct from ivory_black and vine_black in drying and tinting behavior
materials.notes: Synthetic iron oxide. Fast dryer. High tinting strength. Dense, neutral black.

ambiguity_warnings: Product labels distinguish light/medium/deep — these are different hue positions, not different pigments
Some products labelled 'cadmium red' use cadmium-free alternatives — check TDS for PR108 vs. other codes
materials.notes: Opaque, lightfast red. Not available historically before mid-19th century.

ambiguity_warnings: ASTM lightfastness rating is typically poor — relevant for conservation discussion
Distinct from quinacridone_red and permanent_alizarin which are modern replacements
materials.notes: Transparent, cool red. Synthetic dye-based pigment. Known lightfastness concerns.

ambiguity_warnings: Distinct from smalt — smalt is a glass, cobalt_blue is a calcined pigment
Introduced 1802 — anachronistic before that date
materials.notes: Lightfast, somewhat transparent blue. Historical use from early 19th century.

ambiguity_warnings: Distinguish from stand_oil and sun_thickened_oil — all linseed-based but different processing and handling
materials.notes: Most common oil binder. Dries by oxidative polymerization. More prone to yellowing than walnut or safflower.
Less prone to yellowing than linseed. Slower drying. Historically used with blue and white pigments to reduce yellowing.
Pale drying oil. Low yellowing. Slow drying. Common in modern white and light-colored paints.

ambiguity_warnings: Very slow drying — increased risk of paint film failure in thick applications
materials.notes: Pale drying oil. Very slow drying. Low yellowing. High risk of wrinkling and paint film instability in heavy applications.

ambiguity_warnings: Distinct from raw linseed oil in viscosity, drying behavior, and yellowing tendency
Distinct from sun_thickened_oil — produced differently
materials.notes: Heat-polymerized linseed oil. High viscosity. Long, ropy brushstroke. Less prone to yellowing than raw linseed. Good film strength.

ambiguity_warnings: Liquin, Galkyd, and similar brand names are specific alkyd products — do not generalize across brands
materials.notes: Synthetic resin-based drying medium. Accelerates drying. Modern material — anachronistic in historical context.

ambiguity_warnings: As varnish vs. as painting medium — different usage contexts with different conservation implications
materials.notes: Natural resin from tropical trees. Common as final varnish and as painting medium in some traditions.

ambiguity_warnings: Distinct from mineral spirits / white spirit — different solvent chemistry
Oil of spike lavender is a different solvent with distinct properties
materials.notes: Distilled from pine resin. Traditional solvent. Faster evaporating than mineral spirits.

ambiguity_warnings: Odorless mineral spirits is a refined variant — distinguish when handling differences are relevant
materials.notes: Petroleum-derived solvent. Common modern substitute for turpentine.
